Axiom 5 (Well-ordering property)
The well-ordering property of N states that if S ‚äÇN then there exists an x‚ààS such that x‚â§y for all y ‚ààS.
In other words, there is always a smallest element.
Note that this is an axiom, and thus we have to assume this without proof.

This concept was invented by Pascal in 1665. Let P(n) be a statement depending on n‚ààN. Assume that

2. (Inductive step) if P(m) is true then P(m+1) is true.
Then, P(n) is true for all n‚ààN.

Cardinality
Question 10. When do two sets have the same size?
Cantor answered this question in the 1800s, stating that two sets have the same size when you can pair each
element in one set with a unique element in the other.
Definition 11 (Cardinality)
We state that two sets A and B have the same cardinality if there exists a bijection f :A‚ÜíB.

Question 14. Is anything bigger than N?
If A is a set then P(A)={B |B ‚äÇA}. Here are a few examples:
1. A=‚àÖ then P(A)={‚àÖ}.
2. A={1}, then P(A)={‚àÖ,{1}}.
3. A={1,2}, then P(A)={‚àÖ,{1},{2},{1,2}}.
In general, if |A|=n then |P(A)|=2n. This is why we call P(A) the power set of A.
Theorem 15 (Cantor)
If A is a set, then |A|<|P(A)|.
Remark 16. Therefore,
N<|P(N)|<|P(P(N))|<....
Hence, there are an infinite number of infinite sets.
Proof: Define the function f : A ‚Üí P(A) by f(x) = {x}. Then, f is 1-1‚Äì as if {x} = {y} =‚áí x = y.
Thus, |A| ‚â§ |P(A)|. To finish the proof now all we need to show is that |A| = |P(A)|. We will do so through
contradiction. Suppose that |A|=|P(A)|. Then, there exists a surjection g :A‚ÜíP(A). Let
B :={x‚ààA|x‚àà/ g(x)}‚ààP(A).
Since g is surjective, there exists a b‚ààA such that g(b)=B. There are two cases:
1. b‚ààB. If this is the case, then b‚àà/ g(b)=B =‚áí b‚àà/ B.
2. b‚àà/ B. If this is the case, then b‚àà/ g(b)=B =‚áí b‚ààB.
In either case we obtain a contradiction. Thus, g is not surjective =‚áí |A|= |P(A)|.
Remark 17. This is another proof method: casework. If the conclusion for every case is true, then the conclusion
must be true.

Remark 20. In a sense, to be made precise, the set of real numbers is the unique set with all of the algebraic and

Question 29. Last time we stated that Q was an example of a field, but what is a field?
Definition 30 (Field)
A set F is a field if it has two operations: addition (+) and multiplication (¬∑) with the following properties.
A1) If x,y ‚ààF then x+y ‚ààF.
A2) (Commutativity) ‚àÄx,y ‚ààF, x+y =y+x.
A3) (Associativity) ‚àÄx,y,z ‚ààF, (x+y)+z =x+(y+z).
A4) ‚àÉ an element 0‚ààF such that 0+x=x=x+0.
A5) ‚àÄx‚ààF, ‚àÉy ‚ààF such that x+y =0. We denote y =‚àíx.
M1) If x,y ‚ààF, then x¬∑y ‚ààF.
M2) (Commutativity) ‚àÄx,y ‚ààF, x¬∑y =y¬∑x.
M3) (Associativity) ‚àÄx,y,z ‚ààF, (x¬∑y)¬∑z =x¬∑(y¬∑z).
M4) ‚àÉ1‚ààF such that 1¬∑x=x=x¬∑1 for all x‚ààF.
M5) ‚àÄx‚ààF \{0}, ‚àÉx‚àí1 such that x¬∑x‚àí1 =1.
D) (Distributativity) ‚àÄx,y,z ‚ààF, (x+y)z =xz+yz.
These may seem like trivial properties, but consider the following non-example: Z. Z fails M5)‚Äì multiplicative
inverses do not exist in the integers.
